content: This systematic review synthesizes evidence from 19 sources examining how damp heat aging affects encapsulant adhesion strength in flexible electronics, with particular attention to the uncharacterized DVS-PEI material class. Under combined thermal-humid exposure at 95°C, adhesive joint tensile strength decreased by 54.4%, nearly double the 31.3% reduction from moisture absorption alone [6], following Arrhenius kinetics proportional to interfacial moisture concentration [3]. Thermal cycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) produced no statistically significant effect on interfacial mechanics (p > 0.05) [4], confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver. EVA at the glass interface degrades preferentially, reaching levels equivalent to 15+ years of field exposure after 10,000 hours of accelerated aging [13]. Industry data reveal that only 69% of module configurations pass damp heat qualification [16], with 15 manufacturers experiencing failures. Formulation optimization identifies 24% VA content in PEVA [15] and 84–90% gel content in EVA [10] as durability optima, while POE demonstrates superior adhesion stability over EVA [5]. A threshold debonding energy of 160 J/m² is proposed for encapsulant interfaces [10]. The most critical gap identified is the near-complete absence of DVS-PEI-specific damp heat aging data across all dimensions—kinetics, failure modes, formulation sensitivity, and field correlation.
